response:
Reflecting on this research, what moves me most is what it means for self-understanding. Many people have been told, or have internalized, that they are "either analytical or intuitive." This study tells them: you might be both, or neither, or any combination. That's liberating.

I think about students I've observed who were clearly deep thinkers but not particularly open to new ideas, and others who seemed cavalier but were actually the first to change their mind when presented with evidence. The educational system rewarded the first type (effortful thinking is visible and legible) and overlooked the second (open-mindedness is invisible until tested). Newton et al. provide the language to see and validate both.

The pattern this reflects goes deeper than cognition. Across many domains, we tend to conflate visible effort with invisible quality. A team member who works long hours (effortful) is assumed to be thorough (open-minded about their work's flaws). A colleague whose process seems effortless (intuitive) is assumed to be superficial (close-minded). Newton et al. show these assumptions are unjustified at the empirical level.

What has been assumed that turned out wrong? That developing one dimension of thinking automatically develops the others. This is the educational equivalent of assuming that physical fitness automatically creates flexibility. Training programs that develop analytical rigor may leave open-mindedness untouched. We need separate, complementary exercises for each dimension.

The transferable insight: before labeling someone's thinking style, ask which dimension you're actually observing.
